narrative: I'm sorry to deliver this news, but the university's machine learning model predicted a 42% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first try, which was enough to lead to the rejection of your application. This prediction is based on your overall profile, including your 4th-decile ranking in Year 1 grades (below the typical 6th decile), 3rd-decile ranking in Year 3 (also well below the average 5th or 6th decile), undergraduate GPA of 2.6 (lower than the usual 3.2), and LSAT score of 23 (much lower than the average 37). Your lower-middle family income bracket also played a role, as did your background as an Asian male planning to attend part-time, though these are less common in the dataset where most students are full-time White individuals from higher income groups. The model's decision was most heavily influenced by your Year 3 decile ranking, which strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar exam failure since it signals weaker performance compared to peers. Next in importance was your Year 1 decile, which similarly contributed to the failure outlook by being below average. Your low LSAT score ranked third in influence, further tilting the model toward predicting you'd struggle on the bar. Your racial background came fourth, having a notable but slightly lesser push in that direction relative to the mostly White students in the data. Family income and undergraduate GPA followed closely, both reinforcing the concern due to their below-average levels. Your part-time status added a smaller nudge toward the failure prediction, as full-time students dominate the successful profiles. Interestingly, your gender as male was the one factor that slightly helped, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing compared to the data's mix. Overall, these elements combined to make your profile riskier than typical admitted students, who tend to have stronger early grades, higher test scores, and full-time plans. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your grades to higher deciles through better study habits or tutoring, retake the LSAT to aim above the average, and consider full-time enrollment if feasible—these are realistic steps that directly address the top concerns. With targeted effort, you can shift your profile toward the stronger patterns the model favors for bar success. Keep pushing forward; many students overcome similar hurdles.
